text: The surgeon dissects the greater omentum from the transverse colon, successfully entering the lesser sac.
The surgeon mobilizes the greater curvature of the stomach, separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon dissects down to the right gastroepiploic vessel and uses a vascular stapler to transect it.
The surgeon circumferentially dissects the right gastric artery and seals it using a vessel sealer.
The surgeon uses a vascular load stapler to complete the transection of the right gastric artery, ensuring the vessel is securely sealed.
The surgeon circumferentially dissects the postpyloric duodenum, revealing the surrounding anatomical structures.
The surgeon uses a sixty-five blue load stapler to transect across the postpyloric duodenum, completing the division of this section.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to dissect and take the stomach and lymphatic tissues, extending the dissection towards the left gastric region.
The surgeon circumferentially dissects the left gastric vein and secures it using a clipper plier and a vessel sealer.
The surgeon circumferentially dissects the left gastric artery at its root and uses a vascular load stapler to transect the artery.
The surgeon transects the body of the stomach at the level of the left gastric artery using a surgical stapler, completing the separation of the stomach from the surrounding structures.
The surgeon uses a stapler to remove approximately eighty percent of the stomach, completing the transection and resection process.
The remaining intact portion of the stomach fundus is visibly supplied by short gastric arteries.
The specimen is removed through the gel port, and the proximal margin is sent for frozen section analysis, which returns negative.
Using laparoscopic technique, the surgeon creates the jejuno-jejunostomy as part of the Roux-en-Y procedure, connecting two segments of the jejunum.
The surgeon measures and runs fifty centimeters of the biliary pancreatic limb from the ligament of Treitz, followed by a hundred centimeters of the Roux limb.
The surgeon creates the jejuno-jejunostomy using a stapler, completing the anastomosis in a standard fashion.
The surgeon uses a robotic technique to create the gastrogenostomy as part of the Roux-en-Y procedure, manipulating the gastric tissue with precision.
The surgeon brings the Roux limb to the remnant stomach, preparing for the next step in the Roux-en-Y procedure.
The surgeon uses a blue load stapler to anastomose the posterior walls of the jejuno-jejunostomy in the Roux-en-Y procedure.
The surgeon closes the anterior wall of the anastomosis using a running 2-0 Vicryl suture, completing the closure with precise stitching.
The surgeon reinforces the jejuno-jejunostomy with a running two-oh V-lock suture, securing the anastomosis with visible precision.
Following the completion of the anastomosis, the surgeon performs an air leak test on the gastrogenostomy site using a robotic technique, confirming the absence of any air leaks.
